participants.name: A (6tks25)
participants.professional_persona: A self-taught AI developer who completed the AI Apprenticeship Programme (AIAP) after a career break. She now works at a health-tech startup building vision-based diagnostic tools for polyclinics.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Strong in computer vision, medical image segmentation using U-Net, and deploying models in HIPAA-compliant environments. Also skilled in stakeholder communication and agile project management.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Aspires to lead a cross-functional team that brings an AI-powered medical device through regulatory approval within five years.

participants.name: Mei (90ha4l)
participants.professional_persona: Mei Ling is a research engineer at a government-linked AI lab developing NLP solutions for public services. She previously worked in a bank's data science team.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Skilled in transformer-based language models, chatbot design, and low-resource language processing. She has deployed models for Singlish understanding.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: To build AI that serves the public good, especially for multilingual communities, and to pursue a part-time PhD in computational linguistics.

initial_posts.narrative: As an AI practitioner who went through the AIAP, I see the government's commitment as substantial but practically constrained. The policy states over S$500 million for AI R&D, which I take as the total funding. The co-funding cap of S$330,000 per 100E project is clearly stated. For compute, I estimate about 500,000 GPU hours available in 2023 based on the need to support multiple experiments and the fact that most compute goes to industry use. For trainers, I estimate around 80 personnel, drawing from my experience in the AIAP where there were about 10-15 trainers per cohort and multiple cohorts running simultaneously, plus additional experts from AISG and partner organizations.
evidence: The policy explicitly commits over S$500 million for AI R&D through AI Singapore, which I take as the total funding figure.
The 100 Experiments programme clearly states co-funding up to S$330,000 per project, as mentioned in the resource logic.
Based on my AIAP experience, compute resources are shared across many projects; 500,000 GPU hours is a reasonable annual allocation for a national programme supporting dozens of experiments.

From my training cohort, I recall about 10-15 trainers per intake; with multiple intakes and additional industry experts, 80 trainers is a plausible estimate for the programme's active personnel in 2023.

initial_posts.narrative: Given the S$500 million total funding and 80 trainers, I expect the 100E programme to conduct about 120 enterprise outreach sessions in 2023. This is based on my experience with SMEs Go Digital, where we saw roughly 10 outreach events per month across sectors, scaled to the national level. For researcher matchmaking, with 30 events, I estimate one per week on average, pairing researchers with enterprise challenges from the outreach pipeline. Training workshops: with 80 trainers, each conducting roughly 0.75 workshops per month over 12 months yields about 60 workshops, focusing on AI tools and problem framing. Platform onboarding: I estimate 200 assists, reflecting the need to help enterprises and researchers get onto the AI experimentation platform, given the compute resources and co-funding available. My estimates are grounded in the resource constraints and the need to generate 100 use cases in 100 days, which requires intensive activity.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The group discussed how to translate S$500 million funding and 80 trainers into concrete activities for the 100E programme, focusing on generating 100 use cases in 100 days. There was consensus on the need for targeted, quality-driven outreach and careful curation in matchmaking, with estimates clustering around 100-120 enterprise outreach sessions, 25-30 researcher matchmaking events, 50-60 training workshops, and 150-200 platform onboarding assists. Disagreement centered on the pace of activities: some argued for higher volumes (120 outreach, 30 matchmaking, 60 workshops, 200 assists) based on national program benchmarks, while others favored lower numbers (100 outreach, 25 matchmaking, 50 workshops, 150 assists) citing constraints from preparation time and selective pairing. The central tendency across all estimates was approximately 110 outreach sessions, 27 matchmaking events, 55 workshops, and 175 onboarding assists.

initial_posts.narrative: From my perspective as a CTO who has navigated AI adoption in a traditional retail business, I see the 100E programme as a practical bridge between research and real-world needs. Given the S$500 million funding and 80 trainers, the activities phase generated about 108 outreach sessions and 27 matchmaking events, which should yield around 80 approved projects. Not every outreach leads to a viable proposal—many enterprises, like my own, need time to define problem statements that fit the co-funding cap of S$330,000. Of those approved, I estimate about 60 prototypes are successfully built, as some projects may stall due to technical complexity or data issues. The AI Apprenticeship Programme, with 80 trainers and 54 workshops, likely produces about 120 trainees completing the programme, considering the 9-month duration and typical dropout rates. Finally, around 70 enterprises end up with at least one AI solution, as some enterprises may have multiple projects or prototypes but not all reach a deployable stage. My estimates are conservative because I've seen firsthand how challenging it is for small enterprises to fully commit to AI projects without strong internal support.
evidence: From my experience with SMEs Go Digital, only about 70-80% of outreach sessions result in viable project proposals, so 108 outreach sessions lead to roughly 80 approved projects.

The co-funding cap of S$330,000 per project and the need for careful matching between researchers and enterprises means some approved projects won't yield prototypes, so I estimate 60 out of 80 succeed.
With 80 trainers and 54 workshops, the AIAP's 9-month programme typically sees about 60-70% completion rate, translating to around 120 trainees finishing in 2023.
